data.title: Bessent OK with Fed holding off on interest rate cuts at next meeting
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Tuesday shared no objections to the Federal Reserve holding off on cutting interest rates at its meeting later this month amid economic turbulence and the Iran war.

“I think now that we have to wait and see; we have to wait and see what happens to the economy,” Bessent said at the Semafor World Economy Conference in Washington, D.C.

The Treasury Secretary said going into January and coming out of February the economy was “very strong.”

Bessent assured the crowd that energy and gas price spikes would soon ease although the U.S. and Iran have yet to secure a peace deal.

“I think at a point this war will end and things will normalize – and I think the Fed is doing the right thing by sitting and watching,” he said, according to Semafor. 

“I would be shocked if, for instance, the [European Central Bank] hiked, although I do have to say that many European countries, [like] the U.K., [and] many Asian countries are subsidizing demand, which we haven’t done in the U.S.,” he added.

He later suggested the Fed would soon need to reduce interest rates following its last cut in December, noting the country would have “some make-up to do from this quarter” but touted the fact that the war hasn’t weakened the U.S. dollar.

“The impetus here is they will need to cut rates…[but] if they want to wait for some clarity, I understand that,” Bessent told reporters during a roundtable after his remarks at the Semafor conference, according to Politico.

U.S. talks with Iran are expected to resume later this week after negotiations led by Vice President Vance last week were shattered and resulted in his early return from Pakistan. 

On Tuesday, the Treasury warned that it is prepared to deploy secondary sanctions against foreign financial institutions that support Iran as the U.S. military upholds a blockade in the Strait of Hormuz.

Amid conflict resolution efforts, the Trump administration is working to usher in a new Federal Reserve Chair, Kevin Warsh, who is expected to lower interest rates, as recommended by President Trump and Bessent. 

Warsh is expected to start in May at the conclusion of Fed Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s term. 

The president has frequently criticized Powell for not lowering interest rates further.

data.title: Georgia Republican slams Trump's AI Jesus post, Iran rhetoric
data.description:
Rep. Austin Scott (R-Ga.) on Tuesday criticized President Trump both for threatening to wipe out Iranian civilization and for a more recent post depicting himself as Jesus.

“I wish I could say this in a private conversation, but I can’t. So I do want to say it: the Easter Sunday post about annihilating a civilization is not okay. It’s not,” said Scott, referencing a Truth Social post in which the president said “a whole civilization will die tonight” unless “something revolutionarily wonderful” occurs.  

“And posting a picture portraying yourself as Jesus is not okay,” added Scott.

“And so I would just advise all of the people at the White House that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom. And if you read Proverbs, you would know that, and Galatians will caution you that God will not be mocked.”

Scott is the latest voice in a chorus of conservatives who have criticized Trump for posting the AI image, which depicted the president as Jesus healing the sick.

The image, posted Sunday on Truth Social, showed him in a religious garb with his hand on a sick man’s head. It displayed him surrounded by his followers, an American flag, eagles and the Statue of Liberty, along with other figures in the sky. The post was eventually deleted.

Trump shared the post shortly after posting a lengthy statement criticizing Pope Leo XIV, who has been a vocal opponent of the president’s war with Iran.

Trump on Monday said he thought the photo was depicting him as a doctor.

“I did post it, and I thought it was me as a doctor, and had to do with the Red Cross, as a Red Cross worker there, which we support,” Trump told reporters outside of the Oval Office.

data.title: Congress reckons with second 'Me Too' wave as Swalwell, Gonzales resign
data.description:
A second wave of the “Me Too” movement is dominating Capitol Hill, headlined by sexual misconduct allegations that have so far led to former Reps. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) and Tony Gonzales (R-Texas) departing Congress under threat of expulsion.

The swift expulsion threats and ultimate resignations showcase the increasing anger and frustration among a vocal group of female lawmakers who say the problem of sexual misconduct in the Capitol is more widespread than what’s been made public. And they raise questions about whether the moment will be a reckoning that leads to reforms around sexual misconduct policies or a blip defused by Swalwell and Gonzales’s quick exits.

“There are many young women over the last few months that have come forward about multiple MALE members of congress, both Democrat and Republican and the leadership on both sides has done nothing,” Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.), who has been one of the lawmakers at the forefront of demanding swift action against the members accused of sexual misconduct in recent months, posted on X. “I am not going to act like it is fine. This is NOT okay.”

A decade ago, the Me Too movement that increased public awareness of and repercussions for sexual misconduct and assault led to resignations of high-profile lawmakers and changes in Congress’s internal rules.

Former Rep. John Conyers (D-Mich.), who was the ranking member on the House Judiciary Committee, resigned in December 2017 after reports revealed a taxpayer-funded $27,000 sexual harassment settlement in 2015 to a former staffer who said she was fired after rejecting his sexual advances, and other staffers alleged he asked for sexual favors. And Sen. Al Franken (D-Minn.) resigned in December 2017 over allegations of groping and other sexual misconduct, even as he disputed some accusations and said he remembered some interactions differently.

The House in 2018 changed its rules for how to handle allegations of sexual misconduct, including adding a new policy explicitly prohibiting lawmakers from having sexual relationships with their employees. Another change required lawmakers to reimburse the Treasury Department for settlements paid over allegations against them.

But despite that rule change, both Swalwell and Gonzales were accused of engaging in sexual relationships with a staff member. 

Gonzales last month admitted to having an affair with a staff member who later died after setting herself on fire, ending his reelection bid weeks after the San Antonio Express-News reported text messages corroborating the affair. An unnamed staffer told The San Francisco Chronicle in a story published Friday that she had a sexual relationship with Swalwell and that he sexually assaulted her on two occasions.

Swalwell in his resignation statement said he had made “mistakes in judgement,” without specifying what those were, but said he would “fight the serious allegations against me.”

Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.), who was friends with Swalwell but quickly called on him to be expelled in light of the sexual assault and harassment allegations against the California congressman, said Tuesday that Congress will have to reevaluate its policies and procedures surrounding sexual misconduct.

“The legislation that happened after Me Too definitely has to go and be enforced, because clearly it didn’t work,” Gallego said in a press conference in his office Tuesday, also expressing regret for his friendship with Swalwell and denying knowledge of the former lawmaker’s wrongdoing.

“We have to go back and make that better because clearly, there’s holes in this,” Gallego said. “We haven’t created an environment through the legislation to make women, especially staffers, feel like they could come and talk to somebody and not have any repercussions.”

Swalwell and Gonzales’s resignations also showcase shifting norms and attitudes among lawmakers for how much due process one of their colleagues should be afforded before being reprimanded or forced out. A wave of lawmakers in both parties quickly said they would support expelling both Swalwell and Gonzales from the House without waiting for the House Ethics Committee to evaluate the allegations — a process that has taken at least months and even years in the past.

It may have been easier for members to evaluate the allegations of misconduct with staffers as a clear violation of House Ethics rules worthy of swift action without a lengthy process, though, than it will be for them to evaluate other accusations of misconduct by non-staffers.

Rep. Cory Mills (R-Fla.) is another member who several colleagues have said should resign or face expulsion as he is being investigated by the House Ethics Committee for allegations of dating violence, campaign finance violations and more. 

Mills’s former girlfriend obtained a restraining order against him last year for harassment following their break-up. And in February 2025, police investigated a physical altercation between Mills and another simultaneous girlfriend in his Washington, D.C., residence, but Mills and the alleged victim later denied that a physical altercation took place. The police department transmitted a report to the U.S. attorney’s office, but Mills was not prosecuted and the matter is no longer being investigated.

Mills told The Hill’s partners at NewsNation on Tuesday that he was not hearing anything about a vote to expel him.

“I don’t even fall into the category of Swalwell or Gonzales,” Mills said “One, I’m not married, so there’s one thing.” (Mills was separated from his wife and in divorce proceedings at the time the restraining order was granted.) “Two, I’ve never sexually harassed and or had any complaints by any staffers or interns on the Hill. It’s just not even a fair comparison. This is obviously a political democratic tit for tat.”

“I had never been arrested, had never gone to any proceedings, and it was essentially just something where it was a bad break up. It’s just interesting kind of seeing how you’re guilty by accusation as opposed to the way the rule of law truly works,” Mills said.

data.title: DeSantis signals possible redistricting special session delay
data.description:
Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) signaled on Tuesday that a special session focused on redistricting expected to kick off Monday could be delayed, but maintained that lawmakers will convene “one way or another.”

“I have had people say, you know, ‘They still haven’t done a budget. There’s this, there’s this. Is that — could you do a little bit different timing?’ The answer is, I haven’t made any decisions on that,” DeSantis said during an unrelated press conference in Tallahassee on the renaming of an airport. 

“It’s possible you could do a little tweak, but you can’t really push it very far. You got to get it done probably within the next couple weeks,” he said. “And so that will happen one way or another. Maybe on the date that was called, if they, you know, some folks have asked maybe a little bit more time.”

“Maybe you do a few days or a week, but not more than that,” he added.

Florida lawmakers are expected to convene next week for a special session to consider redrawing the Sunshine State’s maps. Some Republicans have previously suggested the party could seek to redraw the congressional lines to net up to five seats for the party. 

Lawmakers are also in the midst of budget negotiations, though lawmakers have been unable to reach an agreement.

GOP lawmakers have expressed concerns that gerrymandering the state, where the party holds a 20-8 majority, too aggressively could create a dummymander benefiting Democrats. This would be catastrophic for the Republicans as they seek to maintain their House majority in a midterms environment that’s shaping up to mirror the 2018 “blue wave.”

The state Constitution also has clear anti-gerrymandering language, which would complicate the GOP’s efforts to pass any new map.  

DeSantis has avoided saying that lawmakers should be redrawing the map for partisan gains, instead saying a looming decision from the Supreme Court on the Voting Rights Act compels the state to redistrict.

He’s also pointed to the state’s population growth as another reason, though lawmakers would only have access to 2020 data if they redistrict this year. 

Florida is seen as Republicans’ last chance at redrawing a new map ahead of November. Virginia is holding a referendum next week, asking voters if the lawmakers can temporarily pass a new map before 2030, which would net Democrats up to four seats. 

Candidates seeking to run for Congress in Florida this fall must file before June 12.

data.title: Greene, Khanna debut unlikely alliance in first joint interview
data.description:
Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) and Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) on Tuesday debuted their unlikely alliance in a CNN interview.

Opposing viewpoints have not deterred their professional relationship in seeking justice for victims of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein or voicing their opposition to the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, among other issues, Khanna told CNN’s Kaitlan Collins on “The Source.”

Khanna last week said he was “game” to forming a partnership with Greene after she spoke out against President Trump’s threats against Iran. Both supported the Epstein Files Transparency Act, which Khanna sponsored with Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.).

“Well first of all, more than the policies, I think it’s the way Rep. Greene and I treat each other,” he said. “With respect, not looking at the worst possible thing either of us has said over the last 10 years and trying to sensationalize it, but trying to actually have a dialogue to understand where the other person is coming from, where we disagree and where we could have more common ground.”

“You know, most Americans want to do that with their families, they want to do that with her neighbors,” Khanna continued. “They’re tired of us being as divided and simply looking to score political points.”

Greene said she “completely agree with Ro,” adding that voters expect lawmakers “to make their lives easier, to make their lives more affordable, and they really would like their government to come to a point where it’s seen and not necessarily heard most of the time.”

The Georgia Republican said Americans believe both parties have “fully failed” them and “they’re coming to a point where they’re saying, ‘Do we need an independent party?'” She added that “forward-thinking leaders” will drive the country forward.

A November poll released by ABC News found that 68 percent of respondents view the Democratic Party as “out of touch,” along with 61 percent who said the same about the Republican Party. The poll showed that 67 percent of Americans say the country has “gotten pretty seriously off on the wrong track.”

Greene and Khanna disagree over single-payer health care, popularized by Sen. Bernie Sanders as “Medicare for All,” with Greene against it and Khanna, a progressive, in support of it. But Greene said health care “is a real problem.”

“And Republicans refuse to face the issue,” she continued. “You know, they complain all the time about ObamaCare, but they’re unwilling to do anything to fix it, and I think there needs to be a real solution there. I’m not for ‘Medicare for All,’ but I am for a strong solution that can help Americans, and I think that involves real work and it can be hard work. Not one that can be won by our conversation tonight.”

Greene added that “more dialogue” is what Americans are “craving.”

Khanna and Greene also both slammed outgoing Reps. Eric Swalwell (D-Calif.) and Tony Gonzales (R-Texas), who both faced bipartisan pressure to resign. Both congressmen said on Monday that they will resign from the House amid allegations of sexual misconduct, which sparked separate investigations by the House Ethics Committee.

Greene added that she believes more lawmakers in Congress are guilty of conduct “similar” to the actions that Gonzales and Swalwell are accused of. Khanna said he would have voted to expel both of them if he could.

data.title: Joe Biden visits alma mater Syracuse for portrait unveiling
data.description:
Former President Biden on Tuesday visited Syracuse University, his law school alma mater, for an unveiling of his portrait. 

“Whatever my legacy may be, I hope it be said that I never stopped striving for the cause of democracy,” Biden, 83, told the audience of more than 500 people, according to Syracuse.com.

Those in attendance, the outlet reported, included New York Gov. Kathy Hochul (D), Syracuse Mayor Sharon Owens (D) and most of Biden’s extended family, including his wife, former First Lady Jill Biden, and his son, Hunter Biden.

Biden received his juris doctor from the Syracuse College of Law in 1968, five years before he was first elected to the Senate. He received an honorary degree from the university in 2009, after he was sworn in as vice president.

During his time at Syracuse, Biden married his first wife, Neilia Hunter. Roughly six years later, she died in a car crash that also killed their daughter Naomi and injured their sons, Beau and Hunter.

Beau, who also received his law degree from Syracuse, died of glioblastoma in 2015. The former president said Tuesday that “sharing” the university as an alma mater with his late son “is one of the things that makes me remember” him “with such pride,” according to RochesterFirst. 

Syracuse announced in February that a portrait of Biden would be unveiled in Dineen Hall. The portrait will be on permanent display in the law school’s Law Library Reading Room, and the public can view it during normal library hours, the university release noted.

The oil-on-canvas artwork was commissioned by portrait artist Michael Shane Neal, whose works are in the U.S. Capitol, the National Portrait Gallery and the Pentagon, according to Syracuse.

The portrait features the former president donning a navy suit and light blue tie, smiling with his arms folded, according to a photo published by Syracuse.com. The outlet noted that Neal visited Biden in his home to paint it.

Biden, an undergraduate alum of the University of Delaware, delivered the commencement address at his law school in 1994, 2002, 2006 and 2016. He also delivered the university-wide commencement address in 2009, when he received his honorary degree.

Biden has spoken in public sparingly since he was diagnosed with an aggressive form of prostate cancer last May. He completed a round of radiation therapy in October.

data.title: Postal Service union rolls out 'American needs Vote by Mail' ad campaign amid Trump attacks
data.description:
The American Postal Workers Union (APWU) has rolled out a pro-mail-in voting advertisement following President Trump’s recent attacks on the practice.

The ad, titled “America Needs Vote by Mail,” features people in multiple occupations and circumstances, from a flight attendant to a college student to a farmer, discussing their reasoning for voting by mail.

“Vote by mail. Keep it. Protect it. Expand it,” multiple people in the ad say as it comes to an end.

The president has recently ramped-up his fight against mail-in voting. Late last month, he signed an executive order to restrict mail-in ballots, requiring the creation of a list of eligible voters in every state and limiting absentee ballots to voters on that list.

Amid the signing of the executive order, Trump repeated false allegations about expansive “cheating” by way of mail-in voting.

A few days later, Democrats sued the Trump administration to block Trump’s executive order on mail-in voting.

“Our Constitution’s Framers anticipated this kind of desire for absolute power,” Democratic election lawyer Marc Elias said in the complaint. “They recognized the menace it would pose to ordered liberty and the ways in which it would corrode self-government like an acid.”

“And so, to preserve the People’s own sovereignty, they crafted a system of government to resist that threat,” he continued.

data.title: Trump DOJ accuses Biden admin of 'weaponization' of abortion clinic law
data.description:
The Trump administration in a new report Tuesday accused the Biden administration’s Justice Department of using a law historically meant to protect abortion clinics to selectively prosecute anti-abortion protesters.

The report from the Justice Department’s “Weaponization Working Group” accused former Attorney General Merrick Garland of being biased in his enforcement of the Freedom of Access to Clinic Entrances (FACE) Act by aggressively charging anti-abortion activists rather than abortion rights protesters. 

But the report included little evidence to back up its sweeping claims of politically motivated prosecutions and ignored multiple court rulings and jury verdicts that resulted from the charges. 

Critics allege the report mischaracterized a surge in violence, threats and blockades directed at abortion clinics as peaceful protests and expressions of religious faith. 

The FACE Act makes it a federal crime to use “threats of force, obstruction or inflict property damage intended to interfere with reproductive health care services.” It was passed in 1994 amid a rise of blockades and violent acts against reproductive health care facilities and patients, most notably the murder of David Gunn in Florida and the attempted murder of George Tiller in Kansas.  

The law also included provisions protecting crisis pregnancy centers and places of worship; the report said Garland “violated the rights of Americans” by only enforcing the law against anti-abortion activists. 

“Though the FACE Act was supposed to protect both pro-choice and pro-life activities, Biden DOJ senior leadership and Task Force Members provided extensive support to abortion clinics, yet the Biden DOJ often ignored and downplayed vandalism and attacks against pregnancy resource centers or houses of worship,” the report stated. 

The Trump report also alleges Biden’s DOJ “closely collaborated with pro-abortion groups to track pro-life activists’ First Amendment activity” and initiated search warrants and charges on “evidence and dossiers” those groups compiled. 

The report is one of the first acts of acting-Attorney General Todd Blanche, who is under pressure from the Trump administration to aggressively pursue the president’s political enemies.  

 “No Department should conduct selective prosecution based on beliefs. The weaponization that happened under the Biden Administration will not happen again, as we restore integrity to our prosecutorial system,” Blanche said in a statement. 

Ahead of the report’s release, the Justice Department on Monday fired four federal prosecutors involved in Biden-era FACE Act cases. The prosecutors had not been accused of misconduct. 

“DOJ has terminated the employment of personnel responsible for weaponizing the FACE Act who still remained at the department,” the agency wrote on social media.  

The DOJ under Garland brought more than two dozen FACE Act-related cases. Some GOP lawmakers criticized those prosecutions and called for repealing the law, claiming it was used disproportionately against anti-abortion protesters. 

Shortly after taking office last year, Trump pardoned 23 people accused of FACE Act violations, including a group convicted of a 2020 planned blockade of a District of Columbia-area abortion clinic. Protesters bound themselves with chains and locks and physically obstructed clinic staff and patients during the blockade, which was livestreamed on social media. 

Trump’s DOJ has also dismissed several other FACE Act cases and ordered prosecutors not to move forward with future FACE Act investigations. Still, the agency has allowed investigations and FACE Act prosecutions against abortion rights activists to continue.  

Earlier this year, the Justice Department charged 39 people — including former CNN anchor Don Lemon — with FACE Act violations stemming from their involvement in an anti-ICE protest at a church in St. Paul, Minnesota. 

“DOJ’s current leaders’ textbook cruelty and hypocrisy are on full display in this report. They insist on zealous advocacy by career staff in advancing the President’s priorities, while shaming and firing those who did just that in the prior administration,” Stacey Young, a former Civil Rights Division attorney who founded and leads the nonprofit Justice Connection, said in a statement. 

Former Civil Rights Division attorneys on Tuesday accused the Justice Department of cherry-picking from emails and other internal records and deliberately mischaracterizing the factual record.  

Regan Rush, a former Civil Rights Division attorney and current director of Red Line for Civil Rights at Democracy Forward, noted in an article debunking the report that the “overwhelming majority of cases brought between 2021 and 2024 … included serious criminal conduct such as firebombing, arson, bomb threats, and coordinated blockades that prevented patients from entering clinics.” 

“Many of the convictions now being questioned were secured through grand jury indictments and unanimous jury verdicts after full judicial review. Those outcomes reflect evidence tested in court, not political bias,” Rush said in a statement.

data.title: Hegseth again skipping meeting of group arming Ukraine
data.description:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will once again forgo a meeting of roughly 50 countries supporting Ukraine in its fight against Russia, a defense official confirmed Tuesday.

The Pentagon’s policy chief Elbridge Colby will virtually attend the Ukraine Defense Contact Group (UDCG) meeting this week instead of Hegseth, the official told The Hill. 

Hegseth’s absence from the meeting, a frequent occurrence in the past year, makes the U.S. one of the few nations not to send its top civilian defense official to a gathering founded during the Biden administration by then-Defense Secretary Lloyd Austin. 

Politico first reported on Hegseth skipping the meeting, which takes place on Wednesday and will be led by the United Kingdom and Germany. 

Hegseth, who has mostly taken a hands-off approach to providing lethal aid for Ukraine, turned over leadership of the UDCG to the U.K. after President Trump began his second term last year.

The Pentagon chief did not attend the group’s in-person meeting at NATO headquarters in April 2025, instead calling in. And he did not participate in the following meetings in June or in February, the latter of which Colby attended.

Hegseth’s absence is in line with the Trump administration turning its focus away from Ukraine and toward the Middle East, where the U.S. is embroiled in a war with Iran. 

Trump early in his second term was skeptical of providing Ukraine with U.S. military support, halting new lethal aid for Kyiv but honoring shipments of weapons and equipment pledged under the Biden administration. 

He later changed his tune and pressed Russia to reach a peace deal with Kyiv, saying Washington would help Europe send more weapons to the embattled country.

But Trump’s focus on ending the fighting in Ukraine has fallen by the wayside amid the U.S.-Israeli war in Iran, which began more than a month ago with an intense bombing campaign. Washington and Tehran are currently halfway through a two-week ceasefire, but negotiations to end the conflict are at an impasse. 

The Trump administration also has expressed its desire that European allies take on the brunt of providing for its own defenses, even as the president has railed against NATO for refusing to help the U.S. in its war in Iran.

data.title: Top NFL reporter resigns after hotel photos with Patriots coach surface
data.description:
Dianna Russini, a top NFL reporter for The Athletic, resigned from her position at the New York Times-owned company on Tuesday after photos showing her hugging and holding hands with New England Patriots coach Mike Vrabel were published.

Russini, in a letter to Times Executive Editor Steven Ginsberg that was obtained by the Associated Press, stood by her reporting on the NFL, saying she was “grateful” for the outlet defending her publicly after the images came to light.

“In the days that followed, unfortunately, commentators in various media have engaged in self-feeding speculation that is simply unmoored from the facts,” she wrote. “Moreover, this media frenzy is hurtling forward without regard for the review process The Athletic is trying to complete.”

Media speculation about her relationship with Vrabel “continues to escalate,” she said, “fueled by repeated leaks, and I have no interest in submitting to a public inquiry that has already caused far more damage than I am willing to accept. Rather than allowing this to continue, I have decided to step aside now.”

Russini’s contract with The Athletic was set to expire in June.

“I do so not because I accept the narrative that has been constructed around this episode, but because I refuse to lend it further oxygen or to let it define me or my career,” she said.

The photos, published by Page Six, showed Russini and Vrabel holding hands, hugging and lounging in a pool at a high-end resort in Arizona around the time of the NFL offseason meetings there.

The Athletic said it was investigating the reporter’s conduct after the photos were published.

Russini joined The Athletic in 2023 after more than a decade as an anchor and reporter for ESPN’s SportsCenter and is widely regarded as one of the country’s top journalists covering the NFL.
